For Lulu ❤️

02 · 06 · 2026

from Dami 🤍

I.   Poems

Euphoria

watch how, today, a boy hides himself
within the mouth of a paean which will
stick its tongue out to as if to taste butterflies.

my belly is a garland of blooming roses. a collage.
a river learning to comb its hair back.
and euphoria which lays still in a bed of poetry.

when that one voice note in May became a song
that taught her to say my name without a lisp,
i would not have believed it would only take months
to taw the icy pump behind my ribcage //

so that it beat harder to the sound of her
voice, and my face spread itself like the fists of a baby who has just fallen asleep.

now: i admit, squarely, that i am a man
who has become an epilogue. and today,
you should know this plot continues to twist
itself into knots like your hair when you
become too lazy.

Ubong Johnson, MD

Finalist, Sean Hodge Prize for Poetry in Medicine

Bloom

I sit and watch the sun
Spill its orange against petals
Of a blossoming flower,
And I am reminded of
Songs that sneak into
Crevices like a tendril
Clambering to wind itself
Around a stem.

One of such songs stirs me
As soon as my eyes pry open
To meet the morning
And a text from you.

I have relished the gentleness
Of your eyes, and would
Never have believed they
Could harden so quickly against
The littlest things that creep
Past the boundaries of my anxiety.

You make being in public
Much more bearable
Than it should be.
And you make being with
You a concert of pristine songs.

Ubong Johnson, MD

Love, Dami

And when, finally, we have
Taught ourselves the little
Things that make a body become
Soil, I want to bury myself
In a promise that you will
Cook that your sweet rice
For me.

Without the tomatoes, I beg you.
Healthy eating no de carry
Person go anywhere.

Ubong Johnson, MD

Birthday,
